items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: What are James's four characteristics of mystical experience?
items.correct: Ineffability (beyond words), noetic quality (gives knowledge), transience (temporary), and passivity (feeling acted upon by something greater).
items.misconception: Religious experiences are just hallucinations caused by stress.
items.random_wrong: Religious experiences only happen during dreams.
items.why: James was a psychologist describing common features, not assessing truth. Students collapse the question of describing experience into the question of whether God caused it — distinct issues.

items.q: Which is a cognitivist theory of religious language?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Aquinas' analogy. “Hare's bliks” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Falsification was applied to theology in the 1950-51 debate by whom?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Antony Flew. “A.J. Ayer” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Tillich's Dynamics of Faith published?
items.why: The real figure is 1957. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
